The Last Days of the Subway Buskers

By Corey Kilgannon

Subscribe to read more.

For decades, the underground stages of New York's subway have been home to a curious cast of performers — a saxophonist who plays standards by the F train at Bryant Park; a Peruvian flute trio camped out at Union Square; a teenage breakdancing crew that turns the express platform at 14th Street into a moving stage every Saturday afternoon. They are the city's unofficial soundtrack, and according to the Metropolitan Transportation Authority, they may soon be quieter.

Citing complaints about noise and crowding, the MTA began rolling out new "performer-free zones" last month — first on the L line, then on the 7. Riders have noticed.

"It's the only thing that made the commute bearable," said Maria Vasquez, 34, a paralegal who lives in Bushwick and rides the L every weekday. "Now it's just train noise and announcements."

A Tradition Under Pressure

Music in the subway is not new. The MTA's Music Under New York program, founded in 1985, has formally licensed buskers for decades. But informal performers — the ones who turn up unannounced and play for tips — vastly outnumber the licensed ones, and they are the focus of the new restrictions.

According to a 2025 MTA report, the agency received 14,000 complaints about subway noise in the previous year, up from 9,200 the year before. Music accounted for roughly a third of those complaints.

Still, defenders of the buskers say the rules will punish performers for noise they did not make. "It's the screeching trains, not the saxophones," said Linda Goldstein, who organizes Local 802, the musicians' union.

What Comes Next

The pilot zones expire in September. The MTA has not said whether they will be extended, scaled back, or made permanent. In the meantime, performers are reorganising — some moving to commuter rail platforms, others migrating to parks, a few quietly testing whether the rules apply to a single acoustic guitarist sitting cross-legged near the turnstiles at midnight.

"I'll keep playing," said the saxophonist at Bryant Park, who gave only his first name, Earl, and has been performing there since 1998. "If they ticket me, they ticket me. I'm here every day until they stop the train."
